elements.sent-2411-001: We study the reconstruction problem of permutation sequences from their $k$-minors, which are subsequences of length $k$ with entries renumbered by $1,2,\ldots,k$ preserving order.
elements.sent-2411-002: We prove that the minimum number $k$ such that any permutation of length $n$ can be reconstructed from the multiset of its $k$-minors is between $\exp{(\Omega(\sqrt{\ln n ))}$ and $O(\sqrt{n\ln n )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-003: These results imply better bounds of a well-studied parameter $N_d$, which is the smallest number such that any permutation of length $n\ge N_d$ can be reconstructed by its $(n-d)$-minors.
elements.sent-2411-004: The new bounds are $ d+\exp(\Omega(\sqrt{\ln d ))<N_d<d+O(\sqrt{d\ln d )$ asymptotically, and the previous bounds were $d+\log_2 d<N_d<d^2/4+2d+4$.}}
elements.sent-2411-005: Reconstructing a combinatorial object from a limited amount of sub-information is a fundamental problem in computer science.
elements.sent-2411-006: Based on different combinatorial objects, different reconstruction problems have been widely studied due to their applications in bioinformatics \cite{acharya2015string,batu2004reconstructing , information theory \cite{ukkonen1985finding , and DNA based data storage \cite{golm2022gapped,yazdi2017portable,gabrys2019unique .}}}
elements.sent-2411-007: Permutation reconstruction is a variant of the well-known graph reconstruction problem, which arose from the unsolved conjecture of Ulam \cite{ulam1960collection : any simple graph with at least three vertices can be determined up to isomorphism by the multiset of all its reduced subgraphs with one vertex deleted.}
elements.sent-2411-008: For permutation reconstruction, one considers reconstructing a permutation from its $k$-minors, that is, subsequences of length $k$ with entries renumbered by $1,2,\ldots,k$ preserving order.
elements.sent-2411-009: The multiset of all its $k$-minors is called its $k$-deck.
elements.sent-2411-010: In 2006, Smith \cite{smith2006permutation introduced the notation $N_d$, which is the smallest number such that any permutation of length $n\ge N_d$ can be reconstructed by its $(n-d)$-deck.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Raykova \cite{raykova2006permutation showed the existence of $N_d$ and gave the bounds $d+\log_2 d<N_d<d^2/4+2d+4$.}
elements.sent-2411-012: In this paper, we introduce another notation $s(n)$ for given $n$, that is the smallest integer $k$ such that any permutation of length $n$ can be reconstructed from its $k$-deck, or equivalently, any two permutations of length $n$ have different $k$-decks.
elements.sent-2411-013: We give lower and upper bounds of $s(n)$ for large $n$, \begin{equation}3^{0.811\times\log_3^{1/2}(n+1)}\le s(n)\leq 2\lceil\sqrt{(n-2)\ln(n-3)}\rceil +2,\end{equation} which imply much better bounds of $N_d$, \begin{equation}d+\exp(\Omega(\sqrt{\ln d}))<N_d<d+O(\sqrt{d\ln d})\end{equation} for large $d$.
elements.sent-2411-014: We also provide a feasible algorithm which assists to determine the exact values of $s(n)$ for $n\leq 10$.
elements.sent-2411-015: The problem of reconstructing a sequence from the multiset (i.e., the $k$-deck) of all its subsequences of length $k$, was introduced by Kalashnik \cite{kalashnik1973reconstruction in an information-theoretic study about deletion channels.}
elements.sent-2411-016: The main task is to determine the minimum number $k$ such that one can reconstruct any binary sequence of length $n$ from its $k$-deck.
elements.sent-2411-017: The best known bounds of the minimum $k$ are $ \exp{(\Omega\sqrt{\ln n )}$ \cite{foster2000improvement and $O(\sqrt{n )$ \cite{dudik2003reconstruction .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: This deck problem has been extended to matrices~\cite{kos2009reconstruction and general higher dimensions~\cite{zhong2025reconstruction , for which the minimum number $k$ such that one can reconstruct any binary $d$-dimensional hypermatrix of order $n$ from its $k$-deck, the multiset of its sub-hypermatrices of order $k$, is $O(n^{\frac{d {d+1 )$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: The problem of partition reconstruction is to ask for which $n$ and $k$ one can uniquely determine any partition of $n$ from its set of $k$-minors \cite{mnukhin1993combinatorial,cameron1996stories .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Here, a $k$-minor of a partition $\lambda$ of a positive integer $n > k$ is a partition of $n-k$ whose Young diagram fits inside that of $\lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-021: Monks \cite{monks2009solution showed that partitions of $n\ge k^2 + 2k$ are uniquely determined by their sets of $k$-minors, which is best possible.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Cain and Lehtonen \cite{cain2022reconstructing completely characterized the standard Young tableaux that can be reconstructed from their sets or multisets of $1$-minors.}
elements.sent-2411-023: For permutation reconstruction, Gouveia and Lehtonen \cite{gouveia2021permutation showed that every permutation of length $n\ge 5$ is reconstructible from any $\lceil n/2\rceil + 2$ of its $(n-1)$-minors.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Reconstruction of permutations from other types of minors is also well studied.
elements.sent-2411-025: Monks \cite{monks2009reconstructing showed that any permutation of $[n]$ can be reconstructed from its set of cycle minors if and only if $n\ge 6$.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Lehtonen \cite{lehtonen2015reconstructing showed that every permutation of a finite set with at least five elements is reconstructible from its identification minors.}
elements.sent-2411-027: De Biasi \cite{de2014permutation proved that the problem of reconstructing a permutation given the absolute differences of consecutive entries is NP-complete.}
elements.sent-2411-028: The paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-029: In Section~\ref{notations , we give necessary definitions and notations, then give a lower bound of $s(n)$.}
elements.sent-2411-030: In Section \ref{upper , we analyze the relationship between the reconstructibility and some specific functions, and give an upper bound of $s(n)$.}
elements.sent-2411-031: In Section \ref{small , we study bounds and exact values of $s(n)$ for small $n$ by giving an efficient algorithm, and deduce much better bounds of $N_d$ than that in \cite{raykova2006permutation from our bounds of $s(n)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: For positive integers $n$ and $n_1< n_2$, let $[n]:=\{1,2,\ldots,n\}$ and $[n_1,n_2]:=\{n_1,n_1+1,\ldots, n_2\}$.
elements.sent-2411-033: For two sequences $\vx$ and $\vy$, let $\vx\mid \vy$ be the concatenation of them.
elements.sent-2411-034: Let $\S_n$ be the set of all permutations on $[n]$.
elements.sent-2411-035: For a permutation $\vx\in\S_n$, a $k$-minor of $\vx$ is a subsequence of $\vx$ with length $k$ whose entries are renumbered by $1,2,\ldots,k$ preserving order.
elements.sent-2411-036: For example, deleting the first entry of $\vx=25134\in \S_5$, we get a subsequence $5134$ of length four.
elements.sent-2411-037: Then renumber the entries by $\{1,2,3,4\}$ preserving order, we have a $4$-minor $4123$ of $\vx$.
elements.sent-2411-038: The $k$-deck of $\vx$, denoted by $D_{k (\vx)$, is the multiset of all $k$-minors of $\vx$.}
elements.sent-2411-039: If a permutation $\vx\in\S_n$ can be uniquely determined by $D_{k (\vx)$, we say $\vx$ is $k$-reconstructible.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Moreover, if $D_k(\vx)=D_k(\vy)$ for two different permutations $\vx,\vy$ in $\S_n$, we say $\vx$ and $\vy$ are $k$-equivalent, and write $\vx\overset{k {\sim}\vy$.}
elements.sent-2411-042: It can be verified by computer that different permutations in $\S_5$ have different $4$-decks, so $\vx$ is $4$-reconstructible.
elements.sent-2411-043: However, one can check that $13524\overset{3 {\sim 14253$, that is, $\vx$ is not $3$-reconstructible.}}
elements.sent-2411-044: It is easy to see that for any two $\vx, \vy\in \S_n$, $D_l(\vx)=D_l(\vy)$ implies that $D_k(\vx)=D_k(\vy)$ for any $k\leq l$.
elements.sent-2411-045: That is, $\vx\overset{l {\sim}\vy$ implies that $\vx\overset{k {\sim}\vy$ for any $k\leq l$.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: So if $\vx$ is $k$-reconstructible, then $\vx$ is $l$-reconstructible for any $k<l\leq n$.
elements.sent-2411-047: By this fact, it is interesting to consider the following problem.
elements.sent-2411-048: Q: Given $n$, determine the least integer $k\leq n$ such that any $\vx\in \S_n$ is $k$-reconstructible.
elements.sent-2411-051: The parameter $s(n)$ is an increasing function of $n$ by the following result.
elements.sent-2411-052: For any positive integer $n$, we have $s(n)\le s(n+1)$.
elements.sent-2411-056: Since all permutations in $\mathcal{S _n$ have the same $1$-deck, we have $s(n)\ge 2$.. Let $k=s(n)-1\ge 1$.}
elements.sent-2411-057: By the definition of $s(n)$, there exist two distinct permutations $\vx,\vy\in \S_n$ such that $D_k(\vx)=D_k(\vy)$, and hence $D_{k-1 (\vx)=D_{k-1 (\vy)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Then for two distinct permutations $\vx\mid(n+1),\vy\mid(n+1)\in \S_{n+1}$, we have \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} D_k(\vx\mid(n+1))= D_k(\vx)\uplus \{\vw\mid k:\vw\in D_{k-1}(\vx)\} = D_k(\vy)\uplus \{\vw\mid k:\vw\in D_{k-1}(\vy)\}= D_k(\vy\mid (n+1)). \end{aligned}\end{equation} That is, $\vx\mid(n+1)$ and $\vy\mid(n+1)$ have the same $k$-deck, which implies that $s(n+1)\ge k+1=s(n)$.
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation} D_k(\vx\mid(n+1))= D_k(\vx)\uplus \{\vw\mid k:\vw\in D_{k-1}(\vx)\} = D_k(\vy)\uplus \{\vw\mid k:\vw\in D_{k-1}(\vy)\}= D_k(\vy\mid (n+1)). \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-060: In order to provide a lower bound of $s(n)$, we establish a mapping between binary sequences and permutations, then adopt the result in \cite{dudik2003reconstruction for sequence reconstructions.}
elements.sent-2411-061: For a sequence $\vp\in\{0,1\}^n$, let $D'_k(\vp)$ be its $k$-deck, i.e., the multiset of all its subsequences of length $k$.
elements.sent-2411-062: Let $s'(n)$ denote the smallest integer $k$ such that any sequence in $\{0,1\}^n$ can be reconstructed from its $k$-deck, or equivalently, any two binary sequences of length $n$ have different $k$-decks.
elements.sent-2411-064: Then there exist two sequences $\vp,\vq\in\{0,1\}^n$ satisfying $D'_k(\vp)=D'_k(\vq)$.
elements.sent-2411-065: It suffices to construct two distinct permutations $\vx,\vy\in \S_n$ such that $D_k(\vx)=D_k(\vy)$, then by the definition of $s(n)$, we have $s(n)>k=s'(n)-1$, i.e., $s(n)\ge s'(n)$.
elements.sent-2411-066: Define a mapping $\Psi$ from $\{0,1\}^n$ to $\S_n$ as follows.
elements.sent-2411-068: Then $\Psi$ maps $\vp$ to a permutation $\vx\in \S_n$ by changing the $m$ ones in $\vp$ into $[m]$ with increasing order and preserving index positions, and changing the $(n-m)$ zeros in $\vp$ into $[m+1,n]$ with increasing order and preserving index positions.
elements.sent-2411-070: It is easy to see that, every $k$ positions in $[n]$ gives a $k$-subsequence $\vw$ of $\vp$ and a $k$-minor $\vz\in \S_k$ of $\vx$ with $\vz=\Psi(\vw)$.. Let $\vy=\Psi(\vq)$.
